content.text: Delegation lets one agent hand off a sub-task to another agent within the same crew. When an agent encounters work that requires a different specialisation — or simply too much breadth — it can invoke a colleague rather than attempting everything itself.
Two built-in tools power delegation. Both are registered automatically on any agent with
(ask a colleague to do work) and
on any agent that should be able to delegate. The default is
to lock an agent to its own tasks.

from crewai import Agent

# This agent may delegate sub-tasks to any other agent in the crew
lead_researcher = Agent(
    role="Lead Researcher",
    goal="Produce a comprehensive research report",
    backstory=(
        "A senior researcher who knows when to call in specialists. "
        "Does high-level synthesis but delegates deep dives to domain experts."
    ),
    allow_delegation=True,  # default — can be omitted
    verbose=True,
)

# This agent only executes what is assigned to it — no further delegation
data_analyst = Agent(
    role="Data Analyst",
    goal="Analyse quantitative data and produce charts",
    backstory="Specialist in statistical analysis and data visualisation.",
    allow_delegation=False,  # stays focused; will not sub-delegate
    verbose=True,
)
How Delegation Works Under the Hood
, CrewAI appends two tool schemas to the agent's system prompt alongside its other tools:
coworker: str, task: str, context: str
Assign a complete unit of work to another agent
coworker: str, question: str, context: str
Ask another agent a question, get a direct answer
of the target agent (case-insensitive). CrewAI looks up the agent by matching
against the provided string and routes the call accordingly.

# The LLM might emit this tool call internally:
# {
#   "tool": "Delegate work to coworker",
#   "coworker": "Data Analyst",
#   "task": "Calculate the year-over-year growth rate from the attached CSV data",
#   "context": "This is for the Q3 2024 investor report. Use the figures in rows 5–20."
# }
#
# CrewAI matches "Data Analyst" -> data_analyst agent and runs it.

from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process

lead = Agent(
    role="Research Lead",
    goal="Produce a complete competitive analysis report",
    backstory="Senior analyst who orchestrates research and synthesis.",
    allow_delegation=True,
    verbose=True,
)
market_expert = Agent(
    role="Market Expert",
    goal="Provide deep market data and competitor profiles",
    backstory="Specialist in competitive intelligence with 15 years of experience.",
    allow_delegation=False,
    verbose=True,
)
writer = Agent(
    role="Technical Writer",
    goal="Transform raw research into polished business reports",
    backstory="Experienced writer who turns complex data into executive prose.",
    allow_delegation=False,
    verbose=True,
)

analysis_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Produce a 3-page competitive analysis for {company} in the {market} market. "
        "Identify the top 5 competitors, their strengths, weaknesses, and market share."
    ),
    expected_output="A professional competitive analysis report in markdown format",
    agent=lead,  # lead will delegate to market_expert and writer as needed
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[lead, market_expert, writer],
    tasks=[analysis_task],
    process=Process.sequential,
    verbose=True,
)

result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"company": "Acme Corp", "market": "cloud storage"})
print(result.raw)
is explicitly defined. The lead agent decides internally to use
to spin up sub-tasks for the market expert and writer. Those sub-tasks are ephemeral — they do not appear in
By default, an agent that receives a delegated task can itself delegate further if its
parameter on an agent also bounds how many reasoning steps (including delegation tool calls) that agent can take before it must produce a final answer.

lead = Agent(
    role="Research Lead",
    goal="...",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=True,
    max_iter=10,    # at most 10 reasoning steps for this agent
)

specialist = Agent(
    role="Specialist",
    goal="...",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=False,  # cannot delegate further
    max_iter=5,              # tight limit for focused work
)
Delegation vs. Context in Sequential Process
mechanism that happens at runtime. Context (
mechanism set at design time. Both move information between agents — but via different routes:
Before task starts — injected into prompt
Full output of listed tasks only
Logged as tool calls in verbose output
already orchestrates task dispatch — effectively it delegates every task. Setting
on worker agents in hierarchical mode allows them to

from crewai import Crew, Process
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

# Manager auto-delegates to workers.
# Workers have allow_delegation=True => can further sub-delegate.
lead_researcher = Agent(role="Lead Researcher", ..., allow_delegation=True)
data_specialist = Agent(role="Data Specialist", ..., allow_delegation=False)
writer = Agent(role="Writer", ..., allow_delegation=False)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[lead_researcher, data_specialist, writer],
    tasks=[main_task],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o"),
)
on both the primary and specialist agents to trace every delegation call, including the constructed sub-task and the result returned.

# In verbose output you will see lines like:
# [Research Lead] Calling tool: Delegate work to coworker
#   coworker: Data Analyst
#   task: Calculate YoY growth from the attached data
#   context: For the Q3 investor report — rows 5-20 of the CSV
# [Data Analyst] Starting task: Calculate YoY growth...
# [Data Analyst] Final answer: Growth was 34% YoY in Q3 2024
# [Research Lead] Tool result: Growth was 34% YoY in Q3 2024
If delegation calls the wrong agent (role name mismatch) or the specialist produces empty output, check:
: verbose output shows what sub-task text the LLM constructed — if it's vague, the specialist produces vague output.
: too low a limit causes the specialist to produce a premature answer.
, the specialist may itself delegate instead of executing.
In production pipelines where agents should only execute their assigned tasks — not spawn ad-hoc sub-tasks — disable delegation explicitly on every agent.

agents = [
    Agent(role="Scraper",   ..., allow_delegation=False),
    Agent(role="Parser",    ..., allow_delegation=False),
    Agent(role="Publisher", ..., allow_delegation=False),
]
# No agent can delegate — the pipeline is fully deterministic
This also reduces token usage and prevents the LLM from constructing unnecessary sub-tasks for simple, well-scoped work.
with 'Researcher', no match is found and the call silently fails. Use specific, unique role names.
: agent A delegates to agent B, which delegates back to A. Prevent this by setting
is what the specialist sees. If the primary agent leaves it empty, the specialist lacks critical background.
: in a large crew this creates delegation storms where every agent tries to hand off work. Set it only on lead/coordinator agents.
: delegation is invisible in the final
if you need a full audit trail of delegation events.
does not assign a new task. It asks a targeted question and gets an answer, which the primary agent uses to continue its own reasoning.

# Internally the LLM constructs a call like:
# {
#   "tool": "Ask question to coworker",
#   "coworker": "Legal Expert",
#   "question": "Does GDPR apply to anonymised telemetry data collected in the EU?",
#   "context": "We are building a SaaS product for EU customers. Telemetry is IP-hashed."
# }
#
# The Legal Expert agent responds as if answering a direct question.
# The primary agent incorporates the answer and proceeds.

legal = Agent(
    role="Legal Expert",
    goal="Provide accurate legal guidance on data regulations",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=False,  # answering questions, not delegating further
)

product_manager = Agent(
    role="Product Manager",
    goal="Design a compliant data collection strategy",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=True,  # can ask legal questions via AskQuestionTool
)
when the primary agent needs a fact or clarification — not a deliverable. This keeps the specialist focused on answering rather than taking over the task.
The default verbose logs go to stdout and are not programmatically accessible. For structured observability, capture delegation events via
In hierarchical mode, the manager agent dispatches all tasks — that is already a form of delegation. Worker agents should generally have
to keep the hierarchy clean and predictable.

# Clean hierarchical setup: manager delegates, workers execute
manager_llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o")

researcher = Agent(role="Researcher", ..., allow_delegation=False)
analyst    = Agent(role="Analyst",    ..., allow_delegation=False)
writer     = Agent(role="Writer",     ..., allow_delegation=False)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, analysis_task, writing_task],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_llm=manager_llm,
)

# If you want a two-level hierarchy where one worker can also sub-delegate:
senior_analyst = Agent(role="Senior Analyst", ..., allow_delegation=True)
junior_analyst = Agent(role="Junior Analyst", ..., allow_delegation=False)

# manager -> senior_analyst -> junior_analyst (two levels of delegation)
Integration-test delegation by running the crew with a task that forces a sub-task and verifying the specialist's output appears in the final result.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew

def test_delegation_routes_to_specialist():
    results = []

    def capture(step_output):
        tool = getattr(step_output, "tool", "")
        if "delegate" in tool.lower():
            results.append(getattr(step_output, "tool_input", ""))

    lead = Agent(role="Lead",       goal="...", backstory="...", allow_delegation=True)
    spec = Agent(role="Specialist", goal="...", backstory="...", allow_delegation=False)

    task = Task(
        description="Research quantum error correction and write a 200-word summary. Delegate the deep technical research to the Specialist.",
        expected_output="200-word summary",
        agent=lead,
    )
    crew = Crew(agents=[lead, spec], tasks=[task], step_callback=capture)
    result = crew.kickoff()

    assert len(results) >= 1, "Expected at least one delegation event"
    assert any("Specialist" in str(r) for r in results), "Delegation should target Specialist"
    assert len(result.raw) > 50, "Final output should be non-trivial"
orchestrates a ring of specialists, all with
. The lead never produces content itself — it only coordinates.

coordinator = Agent(
    role="Research Coordinator",
    goal="Produce a complete multi-domain research report by coordinating specialists",
    backstory=(
        "A coordinator who never writes content directly. Instead, you delegate "
        "every sub-task to the appropriate domain expert and synthesise their results."
    ),
    allow_delegation=True,
)

# Specialists — none can sub-delegate
climate_expert  = Agent(role="Climate Scientist",   ..., allow_delegation=False)
econ_expert     = Agent(role="Economist",           ..., allow_delegation=False)
policy_expert   = Agent(role="Policy Analyst",      ..., allow_delegation=False)
tech_writer     = Agent(role="Technical Writer",    ..., allow_delegation=False)

big_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Produce a 10-page report on the economic and policy implications of "
        "climate change for G7 nations. Cover scientific consensus, economic modelling, "
        "and policy recommendations."
    ),
    expected_output="A structured 10-page report with citations",
    agent=coordinator,
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[coordinator, climate_expert, econ_expert, policy_expert, tech_writer],
    tasks=[big_task],
    verbose=True,
)
A reviewer agent delegates the creation step and then reviews the result — without needing explicit
wiring, because it receives the delegate's output via the tool return value.

reviewer = Agent(
    role="Quality Reviewer",
    goal="Ensure output meets the style guide by delegating creation and reviewing the result",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=True,
)
drafter = Agent(
    role="Drafter",
    goal="Write high-quality first drafts",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=False,
)

review_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Have the Drafter write a 300-word article on {topic}. "
        "Then review it for factual accuracy and style. "
        "Return the final reviewed version."
    ),
    expected_output="A polished 300-word article",
    agent=reviewer,
)
on an agent so it can hand work to colleagues.
(case-insensitive exact match). Use specific, unique role names.
is crew-defined and design-time. Use context for guaranteed data flow, delegation for open-ended orchestration.
on specialist/leaf agents to prevent cascading sub-delegation and to enforce deterministic pipelines.
on all agents to trace delegation calls, inspect sub-task text, and diagnose routing failures.
